[Freddie and Frieda Falcon](/wiki/Freddie_and_Frieda_Falcon "Freddie and Frieda Falcon")

(**BGSU**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Bowling Green, Ohio](/wiki/Bowling_Green,_Ohio "Bowling Green, Ohio"), United States. The 1,338-acre (541.5 ha) main academic and residential campus is 15 miles (24 km) south of [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"). The institution was granted a charter in 1910 as a [normal school](/wiki/Normal_school "Normal school"), specializing in teacher training and education. The university has developed from a small rural normal school into a comprehensive public [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university"). It is a part of the [University System of Ohio](/wiki/University_System_of_Ohio "University System of Ohio") and is currently [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") as R2: Doctoral Universities with [high research activity](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States "List of research universities in the United States").

in two temporary locations at the Bowling Green [Armory](/wiki/Arsenal "Arsenal") and at a branch school in Toledo for the 1914–1915 academic year.[[7]](#cite_note-centpersp-7)[[10]](#cite_note-1914timeline-10) The first honorary organization of the college, the *Book and Motor* was conceived around this time.[[11]](#cite_note-11) It initially enrolled 304 students from [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") who were taught by 21 faculty members.[[5]](#cite_note-Ohc-5) The school graduated its first class in 1915, consisting of 35 certified teachers. University Hall and Williams Hall opened that year, the school's first two permanent buildings. Two years later the first baccalaureate degrees for teacher education were awarded. The university began to invite notable guests to campus during the 1917–1918 semester, including the [Zoellner Quartet](/wiki/Zoellner_Quartet "Zoellner Quartet"), and the [Ben Greet Shakespearean Players](/wiki/Ben_Greet "Ben Greet").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

**Bowling Green State University** (**BGSU**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Bowling Green, Ohio](/wiki/Bowling_Green,_Ohio "Bowling Green, Ohio"), United States. The 1,338-acre (541.5 ha) main academic and residential campus is 15 miles (24 km) south of [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"). The institution was granted a charter in 1910 as a [normal school](/wiki/Normal_school "Normal school"), specializing in teacher training and education. The university has developed from a small rural normal school into a comprehensive public [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university"). It is a part of the [University System of Ohio](/wiki/University_System_of_Ohio "University System of Ohio") and is currently [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") as R2: Doctoral Universities with [high research activity](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States "List of research universities in the United States").

In 2019, Bowling Green offered over 200 undergraduate programs, as well as master's and doctoral degrees through eight academic colleges. BGSU had an on-campus residential student population of approximately 6,000 students[[4]](#cite_note-ataglance-4) and a total enrollment of over 19,000 students as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit). The university also maintains a satellite campus, known as [BGSU Firelands](/wiki/BGSU_Firelands "BGSU Firelands"), in [Huron, Ohio](/wiki/Huron,_Ohio "Huron, Ohio"), 60 miles (97 km) east of the main campus. Although the majority of students attend classes on BGSU's main campus, about 2,000 students attend classes at Firelands and about 600 additional students attend online. About 85% of Bowling Green's students are from Ohio.

The university hosts an extensive student life program, with over 300 student organizations. Fielding athletic teams known as [Bowling Green Falcons](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons "Bowling Green Falcons"), the university competes at the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level as a member of the [Mid-American Conference](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference") in all sports except [ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey"), in which the university is a member of the [Central Collegiate Hockey Association](/wiki/Central_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "Central Collegiate Hockey Association").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1800s–1920: Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1800s–1920: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/BowlingGreenNormalSchool1910.jpg/250px-BowlingGreenNormalSchool1910.jpg)](/wiki/File:BowlingGreenNormalSchool1910.jpg)

First known photograph of the campus, taken in 1910 before buildings were erected.

#### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Background")]

The movement for a public high learning institution in [northwestern Ohio](/wiki/Northwest_Ohio "Northwest Ohio") began in the late 1800s as part of the growth in public institutions during the [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") to meet demands for training and professional development of teachers.[[5]](#cite_note-Ohc-5)[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) During the period, people of northwestern Ohio campaigned for a school in their region to produce better quality education and educators.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) The movement argued that the existing universities, [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_University "Ohio State University") in [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"), [Miami University](/wiki/Miami_University "Miami University") in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford,_Ohio "Oxford, Ohio") and [Ohio University](/wiki/Ohio_University "Ohio University") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens,_Ohio "Athens, Ohio"), were distant and the region lacked a state-supported school of its own.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)

#### Lowry Bill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lowry Bill")]

In 1910, the [Ohio General Assembly](/wiki/Ohio_General_Assembly "Ohio General Assembly") passed the [Lowry Normal School Bill](/wiki/Lowry_Bill "Lowry Bill") that authorized Governor [Judson Harmon](/wiki/Judson_Harmon "Judson Harmon") to appoint the Commission on Normal School Sites to survey forty communities for two sites for [normal schools](/wiki/Normal_school "Normal school"), one in [northeastern Ohio](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio "Northeast Ohio") and one in northwestern Ohio.[[7]](#cite_note-centpersp-7) The commission examined population within a 25-mile (40.2 km) radius of each community, along with [railroad](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") and transportation infrastructure, the [moral atmosphere](/wiki/Morality "Morality"), health and sanitary conditions and site suitability.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)

[Bowling Green](/wiki/Bowling_Green,_Ohio "Bowling Green, Ohio") offered four possible sites and became one of four finalists including [Fremont](/wiki/Fremont,_Ohio "Fremont, Ohio"), [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon,_Ohio "Napoleon, Ohio"), and [Van Wert](/wiki/Van_Wert,_Ohio "Van Wert, Ohio").[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Despite the town being the home of John Lowry, Napoleon was ruled out because the commission found it had numerous [saloons](/wiki/Western_saloon "Western saloon").[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Fremont was eliminated mainly due to the specific stipulations imposed by the [President Rutherford B. Hayes Memorial Commission](/wiki/Spiegel_Grove "Spiegel Grove").[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Bowling Green was chosen on November 10, 1910, over Van Wert in a 3–2 vote by the commission.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) The site located on 82.5 acres (0.334 km2) of primarily rural land and a small town park,[[8]](#cite_note-10timeline-8) nearby railroad and transportation infrastructure, its central location in the region, and Bowling Green's [dry status](/wiki/Dry_town "Dry town") were major factors that the town was chosen by the commission.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) At the same time, the commission chose [Kent](/wiki/Kent,_Ohio "Kent, Ohio") for a school in Northeastern Ohio.[[7]](#cite_note-centpersp-7) Over the years 1911 and 1912, the board of trustees was appointed by the Governor and elected a school president on February 16, 1912.[[7]](#cite_note-centpersp-7) A campus plan was created and $150,000 was appropriated to develop the campus and construct the first buildings.[[9]](#cite_note-1911timeline-9)

#### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Founding")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/BowlingGreenNormalCollege1915.jpg/250px-BowlingGreenNormalCollege1915.jpg)](/wiki/File:BowlingGreenNormalCollege1915.jpg)

Bowling Green Normal School in 1915

The school opened on September 15, 1914, as **Bowling Green State Normal School** in two temporary locations at the Bowling Green [Armory](/wiki/Arsenal "Arsenal") and at a branch school in Toledo for the 1914–1915 academic year.[[7]](#cite_note-centpersp-7)[[10]](#cite_note-1914timeline-10) The first honorary organization of the college, the *Book and Motor* was conceived around this time.[[11]](#cite_note-11) It initially enrolled 304 students from [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") who were taught by 21 faculty members.[[5]](#cite_note-Ohc-5) The school graduated its first class in 1915, consisting of 35 certified teachers. University Hall and Williams Hall opened that year, the school's first two permanent buildings. Two years later the first baccalaureate degrees for teacher education were awarded. The university began to invite notable guests to campus during the 1917–1918 semester, including the [Zoellner Quartet](/wiki/Zoellner_Quartet "Zoellner Quartet"), and the [Ben Greet Shakespearean Players](/wiki/Ben_Greet "Ben Greet").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

On March 28, 1920, a [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"), part of the [1920 Palm Sunday tornado outbreak](/wiki/1920_Palm_Sunday_tornado_outbreak "1920 Palm Sunday tornado outbreak"), damaged three of the school's buildings.[[13]](#cite_note-1920timeline-13) The tornado touched down near Bowling Green and strengthened as it moved into [Ottawa County](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Ohio "Ottawa County, Ohio") where it killed two people in [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa,_Ohio "Genoa, Ohio").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 1920–1940

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1920–1940")]

#### Early expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early expansion")]

Over the next decade the school expanded academic facilities, athletics and student life, as enrollment grew to over 900 students.[[15]](#cite_note-1926timeline-15) On October 28, 1927, Ivan "Doc" Lake, a BGSU graduate and sports editor of the *Daily Sentinel-Tribune*, established the nickname "Falcons".[[16]](#cite_note-1927timeline-16) Lake thought the nickname fit with the school's colors.[[17]](#cite_note-history-17) Prior to "Falcons," sports writers used various other names, including: "B.G. Normals", "Teachers", and the "B.G. [Pedagogues](/wiki/Pedagogy "Pedagogy")".[[17]](#cite_note-history-17) The school achieved the status of college in 1929 when the Emmons-Hanna Bill renamed it to **Bowling Green State College** (BGSC).[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)[[18]](#cite_note-18) At the same time, the college expanded its curriculum through the addition of the College of Liberal Arts, now known as the [College of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Bowling_Green_State_University_College_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Bowling Green State University College of Arts and Sciences").[[5]](#cite_note-Ohc-5)[[19]](#cite_note-a&scollege-19)

#### Great Depression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Great Depression")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/02/BGSUaerial1930.jpg/250px-BGSUaerial1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGSUaerial1930.jpg)

Aerial view of the campus, circa early 1930s

Enrollment levels held steady into the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"),[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) with enrollment surpassing 1,000 for the first time in 1931.[[20]](#cite_note-20) However, in 1933, the [Ohio State Senate](/wiki/Ohio_Senate "Ohio Senate") Welfare Commission proposed a plan to convert the school into a [mental health institution](/wiki/Psychiatric_hospital "Psychiatric hospital").[[21]](#cite_note-30timeline-21) Students, faculty and administrators organized with the Bowling Green community to counter the proposal.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) The Student Protest Committee coordinated with the faculty and administration to organize a campus rally and march through the downtown Bowling Green. Members of the Protest Committee then launched a letter-writing campaign to community leaders throughout northwest Ohio, which helped convince the state legislature that closing the school would be counterproductive. The measure was defeated by a 14–5 vote.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) A few years later, in May 1935, the college was granted university status and changed its name to Bowling Green State University. The university added the College of Business Administration to the existing College of Education and College of Liberal Arts.[[22]](#cite_note-1935timeline-22) Within a year BGSU added master's degree programs in Education, English, History, Social Science and Mathematics.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)[[22]](#cite_note-1935timeline-22) In 1938 the university adopted an official [tenure](/wiki/Academic_tenure "Academic tenure") policy.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 1939 the university began training pilots, led by instructor Mike Murphy at the [Findlay Airport](/wiki/Findlay_Airport "Findlay Airport").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 1939, the university established The Committee for Gifts, Endowments, and Memorials, its first private endowment fund.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### 1940–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1940–1960")]

#### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/22/NavyMarinesatBGSU1945.jpg/250px-NavyMarinesatBGSU1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:NavyMarinesatBGSU1945.jpg)

Navy and Marines performing drills on the BGSU campus during World War II in 1945.

The 1940s, including [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and its aftermath, brought big changes to BGSU. The war caused a drastic decrease in male enrollment and by 1943, the university canceled dances and formals, citing the lack of male students.[[26]](#cite_note-1943timeline-26)

The university continued expanding facilities including its first student union, The Falcon's Nest, and new [cottage](/wiki/Cottage "Cottage")-style dorms for social groups and learning-living communities, and dedication of the [Wood County airport](/wiki/Wood_County_Airport_(Ohio) "Wood County Airport (Ohio)").[[26]](#cite_note-1943timeline-26)[[27]](#cite_note-1941timeline-27) Bowling Green was one of 240 colleges and universities to take part in the V-5 and [V-12 Navy College Training Programs](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program") to supplement the lower enrollment during the war.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The programs offered students a path to a Navy commission, enrolling cadets in regular college courses as well as naval training.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Faculty were added to accommodate the military training programs.[[29]](#cite_note-1945timeline-29)

Student life adapted to the wartime era with efforts such as the War Relief Committee, [blood drives](/wiki/Blood_donation "Blood donation") and [War Bonds](/wiki/War_Bonds "War Bonds") initiatives.[[29]](#cite_note-1945timeline-29) In December 1942 [Sherwood Eddy](/wiki/Sherwood_Eddy "Sherwood Eddy") spoke on campus on topics relating to the [Asiatic-Pacific Theater](/wiki/Asiatic-Pacific_Theater "Asiatic-Pacific Theater").[[30]](#cite_note-30) In July 1944 the university was selected by officials at [Camp Perry](/wiki/Camp_Perry "Camp Perry") as a potential camp for temporary prisoners of war.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### Post-war era expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Post-war era expansion")]

In the post-war era, BGSU constructed temporary structures to keep up with the increased housing demands for veterans and their families. BGSU added 40 trailers to house male and married students in 1945, known as "Falcon Heights". In 1946, the university added 15 steel buildings to house male students in an area near the football stadium that became known as "Tin Pan Alley". By the late 1940s, the student house shortage became so severe that the nearby [National Guard Armory](/wiki/National_Guard_of_the_United_States "National Guard of the United States") and [ODOT garage](/wiki/Ohio_Department_of_Transportation "Ohio Department of Transportation") were converted to house male students.[[32]](#cite_note-huts-32)

The [Federal Housing Authority](/wiki/Federal_Housing_Authority "Federal Housing Authority") provided two wooden barracks, ten trailers, and more steel buildings.[[32]](#cite_note-huts-32) The BGSU [Army ROTC](/wiki/Army_ROTC "Army ROTC") was established on campus in 1948 as enrollment increased dramatically in the [post war era](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93World_War_II_economic_expansion "Post–World War II economic expansion"). The university continued to add academic programs as the enrollment increased during the mid to late 1940s, including the Graduate School in 1948 after Dr. Emerson Shuck led the effort to create school.[[33]](#cite_note-1948timeline-33) In 1948 the university hosted the world premiere of the last play by [Lennox Robinson](/wiki/Lennox_Robinson "Lennox Robinson"), *The Lucky Finger*.[[34]](#cite_note-34) By 1950, enrollment grew to new record highs, with over 5,000 students.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)

1951 saw major changes when Ralph W. McDonald was appointed the fourth president in school history, following the retirement of Frank Prout. McDonald was the first university president from outside Ohio and came to BGSU with a focus on improving teacher education and certification standards. Prior to becoming president, he served as the Executive Secretary of the Department of Higher Education of the [National Education Association](/wiki/National_Education_Association "National Education Association") for seven years. Under McDonald, BGSU reorganized its three colleges to group common departments together within each college.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Reflecting the [Cold War era](/wiki/Cold_War_era "Cold War era"), BGSU added an [Air Force ROTC](/wiki/Air_Force_ROTC "Air Force ROTC") program and a Department of Air Science and Tactics.[[35]](#cite_note-1951timeline-35) BGSU continued to add programs and in the early 1950s added a [Master of Education](/wiki/Master_of_Education "Master of Education") (M.Ed.) and a Master of Science (M.S.) in Education.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) The university constructed new residence halls during the decade, Prout Hall in 1955 and Founders Quadrangle in 1957.[[36]](#cite_note-1950stimeline-36) The new [student center](/wiki/Student_center "Student center") opened in 1958.[[37]](#cite_note-btstudentunion-37)

### 1960–1990

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1960–1990")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Math-Sciences_Building_%28BGSU%29.jpg/250px-Math-Sciences_Building_%28BGSU%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Math-Sciences_Building_(BGSU).jpg)

The Mathematical Sciences Building was completed in 1970 at a final cost of $7.2 million.

The College of Education experienced rapid growth and expansion during the 1960s when the university added various specialized education programs, including majors in [special education](/wiki/Special_education "Special education"), [school psychology](/wiki/School_psychology "School psychology"), guidance and counseling and [vocational rehabilitation](/wiki/Vocational_rehabilitation "Vocational rehabilitation") counseling.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) It expanded arts and music programs when the music department became the College of Education's first "school", renamed the School of Music in 1961.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) Specializations in guidance and counseling were added to the Master of Arts and Master of Education degrees in 1964; as well as a new department for teaching college administration.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) By 1965, BGSU's College of Education enrolled 5,470 students and was ranked the 16th largest producer of teachers in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6) The university added new academic, administrative, and athletic facilities during the 1960s. [Memorial Hall](/wiki/Anderson_Arena "Anderson Arena"), later known as Anderson Arena, opened in 1960.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The new Administration Building opened in 1964 and the William T. Jerome Library opened in 1967.[[39]](#cite_note-1964timeline-39)[[40]](#cite_note-1967timeline-40) [Student activism](/wiki/Student_activism "Student activism") became common in the 1960s, reflecting the various social and political events of the time period. [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") protests were common in downtown Bowling Green and on campus.[[41]](#cite_note-1969timeline-41)

In 1969, a Black Student Union formed to encourage unity, scholarship, leadership, culture and political awareness of African Americans students.[[41]](#cite_note-1969timeline-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The majority of student activism at BGSU was peaceful and Bowling Green was the only public college or university in Ohio to reopen in the spring of 1970, following the [Kent State shootings](/wiki/Kent_State_shootings "Kent State shootings") during anti-war protests.[[43]](#cite_note-1970stimeline-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Bowling Green added two colleges in the early 1970s when the College of Health and Human Services opened in 1973 and the School of Music was elevated to the College of Musical Arts in 1975.[[46]](#cite_note-setting-46)

In addition to the new colleges, the BGSU [Popular Culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture") Center opened in 1970 as one of the first pop culture centers in the United States.[[47]](#cite_note-1970timeline-47) In 1978, the university established the University Honors Program.[[43]](#cite_note-1970stimeline-43)

Throughout the 1970s construction continued, starting with the Mathematical Sciences Building,[[47]](#cite_note-1970timeline-47)[[48]](#cite_note-mathscibuild-48) followed by the Offenhauer Towers in 1971 and Industrial Education & Technology Building in 1972. The Business Building and the Industrial Arts Building opened in 1973 and University Hall received renovations in 1974 that included new seating, an improved sound system, and air conditioning in the auditorium.[[49]](#cite_note-1973timeline-49)[[50]](#cite_note-1974timeline-50) In 1979, the Student Recreation Center and the Moore Musical Arts Center opened.[[43]](#cite_note-1970stimeline-43) In 1970, the Board of Trustees ended an [alcoholic beverage ban](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") on campus. The Cardinal Room, an on-campus eatery, began serving beer. A growing trend in the late 1960s and early 1970s was the development of large [apartment](/wiki/Apartment "Apartment") complexes adjacent to campus.[[47]](#cite_note-1970timeline-47)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

By the 1970s approximately 4,000 students lived in private, off-campus housing. On campus, Darrow Hall became the first [co-ed](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education "Mixed-sex education") residence hall in 1972 with men and women inhabiting alternating floors.[[52]](#cite_note-1972timeline-52)

In 1981, the university hosted Xing-Fang Olu, a geneticist from [Fudan University](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University") to study [cytogenetics](/wiki/Cytogenetics "Cytogenetics"), and sent its own Jong Sik Yoon to teach at Fudan in return. This was the first such exchange in Ohio since the founding of the People's Republic of China.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In 1984, ties were strengthened with Fudan University, along with then [Xi'an Foreign Language Institute](/wiki/Xi%27an_International_Studies_University "Xi'an International Studies University"), following a visit to China by then-University President [Paul Olscamp](/wiki/Paul_Olscamp "Paul Olscamp"), and establishment of more regular exchange programs.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The School of Technology was given college status in 1985 and renamed the College of Technology. The university expanded many of the technology and science facilities during the 1980s, including constructing the [Planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium "Planetarium") and Physical Sciences Building.[[55]](#cite_note-80timeline-55) In 1985, [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") became the seventh president or president-elect to visit the university after [Warren G. Harding](/wiki/Warren_G._Harding "Warren G. Harding"), [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft"), [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy"), [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), and [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford").[[5]](#cite_note-Ohc-5)[[6]](#cite_note-histCoEHD-6)

### 1990–2019

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1990–2019")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/FalconHeightsBGSU.jpg/250px-FalconHeightsBGSU.jpg)](/wiki/File:FalconHeightsBGSU.jpg)

One of the newest residence halls at BGSU, Falcon Heights opened in Fall 2011.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/TheOaksBGSU.jpg/250px-TheOaksBGSU.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheOaksBGSU.jpg)

The Oaks Dining Hall

During the 1990s, the university renovated and constructed many buildings.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [Stroh Center](/wiki/Stroh_Center "Stroh Center"), a 5,000-seat arena to replace the aging [Anderson Arena](/wiki/Anderson_Arena "Anderson Arena"),[[57]](#cite_note-Strohplan-57) opened in 2011, hosting basketball and volleyball in addition to graduation ceremonies, concerts, and other events.[[57]](#cite_note-Strohplan-57)

In 2009, the university began construction on the Wolfe Center for the Arts.[[58]](#cite_note-wolfeSn-58) The 93,000-square-foot (8,600 m2) facility opened in 2011 with performance space, as well as work and classroom areas for art studies of the School of Art, the Department of Theatre and Film, and the College of Musical Arts.[[59]](#cite_note-wolfecollab-59) That same year, BGSU built a $40 million residence hall project that included two new residence halls,[[60]](#cite_note-2dorms-60) one a traditional-style dorm and a second suite-style for upperclassmen.[[60]](#cite_note-2dorms-60) The residence hall project added more than 800 beds.[[61]](#cite_note-setnewres-61) In the fall of 2011, BGSU opened The Oaks dining hall.[[62]](#cite_note-oakspeak-62)[[63]](#cite_note-oaksopen-63)

The Oaks was constructed with sustainable designs that included a hybrid solar and wind power system to fulfill [Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") (LEED), an electric-powered truck to distribute food on campus, and a [rooftop garden](/wiki/Roof_garden "Roof garden"). The building used sustainable and recycled construction materials.[[64]](#cite_note-groundbrkdining-64)

In Fall of 2016 the university began offering a degree in [Mechatronics](/wiki/Mechatronics "Mechatronics") engineering.[[65]](#cite_note-65) In 2016 and 2017, three major renovations were complete to three original campus building, totaling about $70 million.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The former South Hall was re-opened in fall 2016 after undergoing a $24 million renovation and renaming to The Michael and Sarah Kuhlin Center. The building is home to BGSU's School of Media and Communications.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Following a $25 million renovation and restoration, University Hall re-opened in Fall 2017.[[68]](#cite_note-68) It was originally built in 1915 as one of the first buildings on campus. Today, University Hall houses the Office of Admissions, classrooms and [active learning](/wiki/Active_learning "Active learning") spaces.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Also, in Fall 2017, Moseley Hall reopened, following a $21 million restoration and renovation.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Moseley Hall, built in 1916, originally housed the university's [agricultural science](/wiki/Agricultural_science "Agricultural science") program. Today, it features technologically advanced laboratories for chemistry, biology, geology, and medical lab science.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### 2020–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2020–present")]

In June 2020, the College of Health and Human Services created two schools, a School of Nursing and a School of Physical Therapy.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) In July 2020, the College of Business became the first named college on campus when it was renamed to the Allan and Carol Schmidthorst College of Business following a $15 million donation.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) The renaming coincided with the moving to the College of Business from the Business Administration Building to the new Maurer Center, a $44.2 million structure built onto the former Hanna Hall.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Campuses")]

### Bowling Green

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bowling Green")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/BGSU_View_from_Admin_Building.jpg/250px-BGSU_View_from_Admin_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGSU_View_from_Admin_Building.jpg)

The main campus skyline looking east from the Administration Building

The main academic and residential campus is located on the northeast side of Bowling Green. The campus is arranged in a rectangle roughly 1.5 miles (2.4 km) long and 1 mile (1.6 km) wide. It includes over 116 buildings on 1,338 acres (5.41 km2).[[46]](#cite_note-setting-46)

#### Old Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Old Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Mosley_Hall_in_the_Fall.jpg/250px-Mosley_Hall_in_the_Fall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosley_Hall_in_the_Fall.jpg)

Moseley Hall, built in 1916

The oldest portion of the BGSU campus is located in the southwest corner and hosts the original campus buildings. This area offers green space with large trees and [historic buildings](/wiki/Historic_buildings "Historic buildings") built in the early 1900s. Administrative services and classrooms occupy these buildings. Although not part of the historic section, Founders Hall, a large dorm, is located on the Southwest corner. Hanna Hall housed "The Dorothy and Lillian Gish Film Theater and Gallery" prior to its renovation as the Maurer Center. Dedicated to BGSU in 1976, it features early film memorabilia and highlights the careers of both [Lillian Gish](/wiki/Lillian_Gish "Lillian Gish") and [Dorothy Gish](/wiki/Dorothy_Gish "Dorothy Gish"). The theater was renovated and rededicated in 1990. It seated 168 and was home to *Tuesdays at the Gish*, an [International Film](/wiki/World_cinema "World cinema") Series, and a Sunday Matinee Series, which were free and open to the community.[[77]](#cite_note-gish-77) On May 3, 2019, the trustees voted to remove the Gish name from the theater following calls to do so from the Black Student Union and a subsequent task force report, which found that naming the theater after Lillian Gish created a "non-inclusive learning environment" due to her involvement with *[The Birth of a Nation](/wiki/The_Birth_of_a_Nation "The Birth of a Nation")*.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Mike Kaplan, co-producer of *[The Whales of August](/wiki/The_Whales_of_August "The Whales of August")* (1987), Lillian Gish's final film, circulated a petition urging Bowling Green State University to restore the names of the Gish sisters to the film theater. The protest was signed by over 50 film industry figures, including actors Dame [Helen Mirren](/wiki/Helen_Mirren "Helen Mirren"), [James Earl Jones](/wiki/James_Earl_Jones "James Earl Jones"), [Malcolm McDowell](/wiki/Malcolm_McDowell "Malcolm McDowell"), and [Lauren Hutton](/wiki/Lauren_Hutton "Lauren Hutton"), and directors [George Stevens Jr.](/wiki/George_Stevens_Jr. "George Stevens Jr."), [Peter Bogdanovich](/wiki/Peter_Bogdanovich "Peter Bogdanovich"), [Bertrand Tavernier](/wiki/Bertrand_Tavernier "Bertrand Tavernier"), [Joe Dante](/wiki/Joe_Dante "Joe Dante"), and [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

#### Science Research Complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Science Research Complex")]

The Science Research Complex is located on the northwest side of campus. The buildings of the science research complex were built in the mid-60s. They include Mathematical Science, Life Science, Psychology, Physical Science, and Technology (engineering).[[48]](#cite_note-mathscibuild-48)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The Geology, Chemistry and Earth Science departments are located in Overman Hall.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

#### Student life facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Student life facilities")]

Three large residence halls occupy the western edge of campus. Offenhauer Towers consist of a ten-story and an eleven-story tower, connected by a first-floor lobby.[[84]](#cite_note-offenhauertowers-84) Offenhauer shelters a small convenience store.[[84]](#cite_note-offenhauertowers-84) McDonald Hall houses over 1,200 students.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The western edge hosts The Oaks, an eco-friendly dining facility. Falcon Heights, a new residence hall, was added in 2011 across from Offenhauer Towers.[[63]](#cite_note-oaksopen-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/BGsnowJan2009.jpg/250px-BGsnowJan2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGsnowJan2009.jpg)

Fresh snow covers the lawn near the Student Union.

The Bowen-Thompson Student Union opened in 2002 in the west-central part of campus.[[37]](#cite_note-btstudentunion-37) It houses eateries including The Falcon's Nest [food court](/wiki/Food_court "Food court"), [Starbucks](/wiki/Starbucks "Starbucks") and Pub 1910 (previously known as The Black Swamp Pub). Other facilities include Falcon Outfitters, [convenience store](/wiki/Convenience_store "Convenience store"), [computer labs](/wiki/Computer_lab "Computer lab"), meeting rooms, a 250-seat [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater"), ballrooms, and various student lounges.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Central Campus features large lecture halls and classroom buildings. One of the most prominent is the 95,000 sq ft (9,000 m2) Olscamp Hall, which contains 28 classrooms and lecture halls capable of seating a total of 2,000 students. Others include Schmidthorst Business Building, the Education Building, MacLeod Hall (formerly the Math/Science Building), and the Eppler Complex, home to the [Sport Management](/wiki/Sport_Management "Sport Management") department. [Anderson Arena](/wiki/Anderson_Arena "Anderson Arena") is a 5,000-seat arena, current home for BGSU women's gymnastics and former home of BGSU men's and women's basketball and volleyball. Memorial Hall is connected to Anderson and houses the college's [ROTC](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") programs.[[87]](#cite_note-87) [Jerome Library](/wiki/University_Libraries_at_Bowling_Green_State_University "University Libraries at Bowling Green State University") is the main library on campus and the second tallest building at nine stories. Conklin North is another residence hall in the central portion of campus.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Oak Grove [Cemetery](/wiki/Cemetery "Cemetery") is located in the north-central portion of campus.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Bowling Green State University opened the Falcon [health center](/wiki/Community_health_center "Community health center") in 2013 after demolishing the Popular Culture building in 2012.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The Falcon Health Center is located across Wooster Street from the Education Building.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The Health and Human Services building is located near the Library.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The BGSU campus police station as well as counseling services are located in the College Park Office Building on the southern edge. Kohl Hall is a [dormitory](/wiki/Dormitory "Dormitory") exclusive to members of the Chapman Learning Community, Partners in Context and Community for Urban Educators.[[93]](#cite_note-93) A new predominantly freshman dormitory known as Centennial Hall was established in 2011 and is adjacent to the Carillon Place Dining facility.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) The southern edge hosts on-campus [fraternity](/wiki/Fraternity_houses "Fraternity houses") and [sorority houses](/wiki/Sorority_houses "Sorority houses").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Nearby is the Kreischer Quadrangle and the Alumni Mall.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) Kreischer Quadrangle contains four connected halls that operate as separate units.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Kreischer features a late night dining hall as well as a convenience store.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Kreischer-Compton is home to the Arts Village Learning Community for students with interests in dance, art, creative writing, theater, or music.[[101]](#cite_note-artsvillage-101) This community offers members-only classes.[[101]](#cite_note-artsvillage-101)

#### Arts facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Arts facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Jerome_Library.jpg/250px-Jerome_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerome_Library.jpg)

Jerome Library on BGSU's main campus

Arts programs are located to the east of Anderson Arena and [Jerome Library](/wiki/University_Libraries_at_Bowling_Green_State_University "University Libraries at Bowling Green State University") in the east-central area. The Fine Arts Center is home to the School of Art and houses classrooms, a studio, workshop spaces, [art galleries](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum"), a [glassblowing](/wiki/Glassblowing "Glassblowing") studio and faculty offices. BGSU is one of only a few schools that offer degrees in glassblowing. The Moore Musical Arts Center is located along Ridge St and is the home to the College of Musical Arts. Moore includes classrooms, [recording studios](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio"), rehearsal halls, and Kobacker Hall, a large theater where many performances on campus are held.[[102]](#cite_note-102) Moore also includes MidAmerican Center for Contemporary Music, a national center with a focus on the study, performance, creative work and promotion of contemporary music.[[103]](#cite_note-103) The Wolfe Center for the Arts opened in December 2011.[[104]](#cite_note-Wolfeonmap-104) It is the new centerpiece for the Arts, located between Fine Arts and Moore. The 93,000 sq ft (9,000 m2) building houses the School of Art, the Department of Theatre and Film, and the School of Musical Arts.[[59]](#cite_note-wolfecollab-59) It includes classrooms, rehearsal space, performance/theater space, as well as design and office space, the 400-seat Thomas and Kathleen Donnell Theatre,[[105]](#cite_note-105) and a [black-box stage](/wiki/Black_box_theatre "Black box theatre"), editing and digital laboratories, classrooms, studios, faculty space, and choral rehearsal rooms.[[59]](#cite_note-wolfecollab-59)[[106]](#cite_note-106)

#### Athletic and recreational facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Athletic and recreational facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/StrohCenterinterior.jpg/250px-StrohCenterinterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:StrohCenterinterior.jpg)

Interior of the Stroh Center prior to an exhibition game against [Tiffin](/wiki/Tiffin_University#Athletics "Tiffin University").

Most athletic and recreational facilities are located on the eastern half of campus. The Student Recreation Center is a 185,000 sq ft (17,000 m2) facility that includes two [swimming pools](/wiki/Swimming_pool "Swimming pool"), four [weight rooms](/wiki/Weight_training "Weight training"), a [cardio](/wiki/Aerobic_exercise "Aerobic exercise") room, an elevated [running track](/wiki/All-weather_running_track "All-weather running track"), an Activity Center for [aerobics](/wiki/Aerobics "Aerobics") and a large sports center which accommodates basketball, tennis, volleyball, and badminton and other sports.[[107]](#cite_note-107) North of the center lies Perry Field House, a 127,000 sq ft (12,000 m2) athletic facility with a 100 x 60 indoor [synthetic turf](/wiki/Synthetic_turf "Synthetic turf"), four batting cages, and a 200-meter track encircling four courts for basketball, volleyball, or tennis.[[108]](#cite_note-108) The [Slater Family Ice Arena](/wiki/Slater_Family_Ice_Arena "Slater Family Ice Arena") is a 5,000-seat [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") arena that is used by various teams and clubs as well as public use. The rink is also home to the Black Swamp Ice Frogs, a special needs hockey team.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The arena also includes a smaller ice sheet for [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling"), [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating"), [youth ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), and [public skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating").[[110]](#cite_note-110) The Eppler complex is the oldest building on campus for athletics and is the main practice area for [cheerleading](/wiki/Cheerleading "Cheerleading"), gymnastics, dancing and fencing. At one time it housed the original natatorium.[[111]](#cite_note-111) [Doyt Perry Stadium](/wiki/Doyt_Perry_Stadium "Doyt Perry Stadium") is a 28,600 seat football stadium located on the eastern edge.[[112]](#cite_note-112) The [Stroh Center](/wiki/Stroh_Center "Stroh Center") is an on-campus venue for athletics, concerts, commencement, lectures, and numerous campus and community events. The facility serves as the home for the Falcons men's and women's basketball and volleyball programs. Notably, the structure is one of the most environmentally friendly buildings on campus, designed to achieve challenging Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design [(LEED)](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") certification.[[113]](#cite_note-strohcompletion-113)

#### Transportation and safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transportation and safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Orange_bus.jpg/250px-Orange_bus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orange_bus.jpg)

BGSU shuttle near the Visitor Center stop

The campus [fare-free](/wiki/Free_public_transport "Free public transport") bus transit system began in 1990 and runs throughout the campus and surrounding neighborhoods.[[114]](#cite_note-90timeline-114) In 2005 the university started testing [Hybrid buses](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_bus "Hybrid electric bus") on the service's main route.[[115]](#cite_note-hybus-115) The first hybrid bus on the system used a proprietary [diesel-electric](/wiki/Diesel-electric "Diesel-electric") propulsion system, known as a Hybrid Booster Drive (HBD), and was developed by the Electric Vehicle Institute (EVI) within the BGSU College of Technology.[[115]](#cite_note-hybus-115) The system is made up of four routes.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-shuttleroutes-117) It includes major stops at various residence halls, academic buildings and athletic buildings.[[117]](#cite_note-shuttleroutes-117) The campus has a [mobile application](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") that allows the students to track the location of the different buses around campus. This application also gives an [estimated time of arrival](/wiki/Estimated_time_of_arrival "Estimated time of arrival") for each route to reach a given bus stop.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

The Orange Bike program began in 2008 as part of an increase in campus sustainability. The program offers a [community](/wiki/Bicycle-sharing_system "Bicycle-sharing system") [bike rental service](/wiki/Bike_rental "Bike rental") to students to reduce the [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint") of commuters.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

The University Police Department provides 24-hour law enforcement and security, [campus escort service](/wiki/Safety_escort_service "Safety escort service"), motorist assistance, educational programs, and [crime prevention](/wiki/Crime_prevention "Crime prevention") information for the BGSU campus and surrounding areas.[[120]](#cite_note-univpolice-120)

### BGSU Firelands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: BGSU Firelands")]

Main article: [BGSU Firelands](/wiki/BGSU_Firelands "BGSU Firelands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/BGSU_Firelands_2_071_%284411064343%29.jpg/250px-BGSU_Firelands_2_071_%284411064343%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGSU_Firelands_2_071_(4411064343).jpg)

The Firelands James H. McBride Arboretum

The college is located in [Huron, Ohio](/wiki/Huron,_Ohio "Huron, Ohio"), about 60 miles (97 km) east of Bowling Green. BGSU Firelands is a non-residential, [commuter school](/wiki/Commuter_school "Commuter school") that offers [associate degrees](/wiki/Associate_degree "Associate degree") and prepares students for transfer to bachelor's programs or for entry into the job market in technical or [paraprofessional](/wiki/Paraprofessional "Paraprofessional") areas. The college also offers some on-site bachelor's degree programs and general education classes that students can apply for majors at the main campus classes or transfer to another four-year institution.[[121]](#cite_note-firelands-121)

First offered in 1946 in the [Sandusky](/wiki/Sandusky,_Ohio "Sandusky, Ohio") area and later expanded to serve [Erie](/wiki/Erie_County,_Ohio "Erie County, Ohio"), [Huron](/wiki/Huron_County,_Ohio "Huron County, Ohio"), [Lorain](/wiki/Lorain_County,_Ohio "Lorain County, Ohio"), and [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Ohio "Ottawa County, Ohio") counties, extension programs established a foundation for BGSU Firelands, the university's regional campus. The college was established at a site located near [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") in [Huron, Ohio](/wiki/Huron,_Ohio "Huron, Ohio"), when the first building (now Foundation Hall) was opened. In 2003, Cedar Point Center opened its doors on the Firelands campus.[[122]](#cite_note-firelandshist-122) The facility houses a 450-seat divisible public meeting area, smaller conference rooms, a [cyber cafe](/wiki/Internet_caf%C3%A9 "Internet café"), multimedia classrooms, and two [distance learning](/wiki/Distance_education "Distance education") classrooms.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

## Academics and rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Academics and rankings")]

Bowling Green State University offers more than 200 undergraduate majors and confers degrees.[[124]](#cite_note-124) BGSU has full accreditation from the [Higher Learning Commission](/wiki/Higher_Learning_Commission "Higher Learning Commission") (HLC).[[125]](#cite_note-ncahlc-125) Bowling Green has been fully accredited by the North Central Association of the Higher Learning Commission since 1916 and received its ten-year renewal in 2002–2003.[[125]](#cite_note-ncahlc-125) In addition, BGSU has accreditation from the HLC to offer full degree programs online.[[125]](#cite_note-ncahlc-125) The university offers [bachelor's degrees](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), master's degrees, and [doctoral degrees](/wiki/Doctoral_degree "Doctoral degree") through its eight colleges:[[126]](#cite_note-126)

Schmidthorst College of Business[[127]](#cite_note-127) * [College of Education and Human Development](/wiki/Bowling_Green_State_University_College_of_Education_and_Human_Development "Bowling Green State University College of Education and Human Development") * [Firelands College](/wiki/BGSU_Firelands "BGSU Firelands") | * Graduate College * College of Health and Human Services * College of Musical Arts * College of Technology, Architecture and Applied Engineering |

BGSU was ranked 119th on the Top Public Schools ranking by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The university remains a leader in teacher preparation and was ranked 127th among America's Best Education Schools by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Students enrolled in the [College of Education and Human Development](/wiki/Education "Education") may choose majors from among several teacher licensure areas, including early childhood (grades Pre-K to 3), Middle Childhood (grades 4–9), Adolescent-Young Adult (grades 7–12), Special Education (grades K–12), and foreign language (grades K–12). In addition, BGSU continues to have one of the top four programs in the United States for Industrial & Organizational Psychology per *U.S. News & World Report*.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The university is also ranked the most affordable college in Ohio by Business Insider.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

BGSU offered the nation's first Ph.D. program in [photochemical](/wiki/Photochemical "Photochemical") science. BGSU's graduate program in chemistry is ranked 150 in U.S. News & World Report's Best Grad School Rankings.[[136]](#cite_note-136) BGSU also offered the first Ph.D. program in [applied philosophy](/wiki/Applied_philosophy "Applied philosophy").[[137]](#cite_note-137) Due in part to a grant from the National Institute of Standards and Technology, BGSU is building a [digital forensics](/wiki/Digital_forensics "Digital forensics") lab to complement its digital forensics and cybersecurity courses.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The college of Business opened a facility at Levis Commons in Perrysburg, Ohio, for its Professional MBA program. BGSU opened a satellite campus offering MBA classes at [Owens Community College](/wiki/Owens_Community_College "Owens Community College") in [Findlay, Ohio](/wiki/Findlay,_Ohio "Findlay, Ohio"), in January 2013. BGSU is one of only two universities with an airport on its campus.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

### Tuition and graduation rates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Tuition and graduation rates")]

Spring 2019 undergraduate tuition for the main campus costs are [$](/wiki/US$ "US$")379.00 per credit hour for in-state tuition while out-of-state tuition is $711.85 per credit hour.[[140]](#cite_note-140) The prices for incoming freshmen tuition and fees are a 5.9% increase from the 2017–18 academic year in response to state funding policies.[[141]](#cite_note-141) Fall 2019 graduate tuition costs are $445.40 per credit hour for in-state tuition, and out-of-state tuition is $778.25 per credit hour.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

The six-year graduation rate for the university's main campus was 61 percent.[[143]](#cite_note-degreerateforce-143) Bowling Green State University's six-year graduation rate exceeded its predicted rate of 47 percent.[[143]](#cite_note-degreerateforce-143) The university's was named in the top five positive differences between actual and expected graduation rates of similar public universities by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[144]](#cite_note-144) Graduation rates for by race among this group are 60 percent unknown race, 60 percent [white](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 55 percent [Asian-American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 50 percent [African-American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 50 percent [international students](/wiki/International_student "International student"), 48 percent [Hispanic-American](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), and 43 percent [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") students.[[145]](#cite_note-145) Both its part-time MBA program and graduate program in education are ranked in U.S. News & World Report's 2019 Best Grad School Rankings, at 163 and 124, respectively.[[146]](#cite_note-usnews.com-146)

### Faculty and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Faculty and research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/BGSU_Telescope.jpg/250px-BGSU_Telescope.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGSU_Telescope.jpg)

A reflecting telescope at Bowling Green State University

BGSU has a [student-faculty ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 18:1.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The university currently has 1,982 academic staff, including 797 full-time faculty, 312 [adjunct faculty](/wiki/Adjunct_faculty "Adjunct faculty"), and 873 graduate assistant and research staff.[[2]](#cite_note-ncesinfo-2) Since November 2010, BGSU full-time faculty have been represented in collective bargaining by the BGSU Faculty Association, a chapter of the [American Association of University Professors](/wiki/American_Association_of_University_Professors "American Association of University Professors").[[148]](#cite_note-148)

In 1979, American author [James Baldwin](/wiki/James_Baldwin_(writer) "James Baldwin (writer)") taught at BGSU for one quarter as a Distinguished Visiting Professor in the [Ethnic Studies](/wiki/Ethnic_Studies "Ethnic Studies") Department after a month-long stint as [writer in residence](/wiki/Writer_in_residence "Writer in residence") in 1978.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

The [Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") classified Bowling Green State University as a [Doctorate-granting Research University](/wiki/Doctoral_university "Doctoral university") with high research activity.[[150]](#cite_note-Carnegie-150)

The BGSU Center for Sustainability and the Environment conducts research on [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") such as [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy") and wind generation on [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), energy conversion, and using algae to generate [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"). Research in conjunction with the [University of Toledo](/wiki/University_of_Toledo "University of Toledo") created new ways to effectively determine appropriate Ohio windmill sites.[[151]](#cite_note-1stctrforexcell-151)

The "Center of Excellence for Health and Wellness Across the Lifespan" primarily focuses on research pertaining to areas such as physical health, substance use and abuse, mental health, voice and speech science, family and marriage research, and health communication. It houses the first National Center for Family & Marriage Research, established by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services along with the Center for Family and Demographic Research, which received long-term funding by the National Institutes of Health for voice and speech science research.[[152]](#cite_note-ctrforexcellnamed-152)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Bowling Green Falcons](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons "Bowling Green Falcons")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/BGvMIhky.jpg/250px-BGvMIhky.jpg)](/wiki/File:BGvMIhky.jpg)

[Bowling Green men's ice hockey](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons_men%27s_ice_hockey "Bowling Green Falcons men's ice hockey") vs. [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_ice_hockey "Michigan Wolverines men's ice hockey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Russ_Chandler_Stadium_Jason_Link_%2812854154303%29.jpg/250px-Russ_Chandler_Stadium_Jason_Link_%2812854154303%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russ_Chandler_Stadium_Jason_Link_(12854154303).jpg)

A [Falcons baseball](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons_baseball "Bowling Green Falcons baseball") player during a 2014 game.

Bowling Green's athletic teams are known as the Falcons. The university participates in [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") as a member of the [Mid-American Conference](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference"), part of the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision"). In ice hockey, the Falcons are members of the [Central Collegiate Hockey Association](/wiki/Central_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "Central Collegiate Hockey Association"). The Falcons' main rivals are the [Rockets](/wiki/Toledo_Rockets "Toledo Rockets") of the [University of Toledo](/wiki/University_of_Toledo "University of Toledo"). Separated by just 20 miles (32 km) on [Interstate 75](/wiki/Interstate_75 "Interstate 75"), the two schools celebrate a heated rivalry in several sports.[[155]](#cite_note-155) The most well-known of these games is the [Battle of I-75](/wiki/Battle_of_I-75 "Battle of I-75"), a football game held each year in which the winner takes home the [Peace Pipe](/wiki/Peace_Pipe_(college_football) "Peace Pipe (college football)"), a Native American peace pipe placed upon a wood tablet.[[156]](#cite_note-156) The university sponsors 16 athletic teams: men's and women's [basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball"), men's and women's [cross county](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running"), [football](/wiki/College_football "College football"), men's and women's golf, women's gymnastics, men's [ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey"), men's and women's soccer, [softball](/wiki/College_softball "College softball"), women's swimming, women's tennis, women's track and field, and women's volleyball.[[157]](#cite_note-157)

In March 2024, Bowling Green announced that it had hired back Dennis Williams as the hockey team head coach.[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)

The Falcons women's basketball teams had recent postseason success. The team won conference championships in women's basketball in 2005, 2006, and 2007.[[160]](#cite_note-wbballmacchamps-160) At the [NCAA Women's Division I Basketball Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Women's Division I Basketball Championship"), the Falcons lost in the first round in 2005 and 2006, but then reached the "Sweet Sixteen" in 2007.[[161]](#cite_note-161) The 1984 Falcons hockey team defeated the [University of Minnesota Duluth](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Duluth "University of Minnesota Duluth") in the longest college hockey championship game in history, to win the NCAA National Championship.[[162]](#cite_note-162) Former BGSU head football coach [Doyt Perry](/wiki/Doyt_Perry "Doyt Perry") led the Falcons to the NCAA "Small College" Football National Title and undefeated season in 1959.[[163]](#cite_note-163) Several BGSU coaches have gone on to prominent careers. Football coach [Urban Meyer](/wiki/Urban_Meyer "Urban Meyer") went on to great success at the [University of Florida](/wiki/Florida_Gators_football "Florida Gators football"), earning two [BCS National Championship Game](/wiki/BCS_National_Championship_Game "BCS National Championship Game") appearances in a three-year span, winning in [2007](/wiki/2007_BCS_National_Championship_Game "2007 BCS National Championship Game") and [2009](/wiki/2009_BCS_National_Championship_Game "2009 BCS National Championship Game"). He also won the first [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") championship while coaching at [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football "Ohio State Buckeyes football") in [2015](/wiki/2015_College_Football_Playoff_National_Championship "2015 College Football Playoff National Championship").[[164]](#cite_note-164) Hockey coach [Jerry York](/wiki/Jerry_York "Jerry York") became the winningest active coach in NCAA hockey, winning four NCAA National Championships at [Boston College](/wiki/Boston_College "Boston College") in [2001](/wiki/2001_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Ice_Hockey_Tournament "2001 NCAA Men's Division I Ice Hockey Tournament"), [2008](/wiki/2008_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Ice_Hockey_Tournament "2008 NCAA Men's Division I Ice Hockey Tournament"), [2010](/wiki/2010_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Ice_Hockey_Tournament "2010 NCAA Men's Division I Ice Hockey Tournament") and [2012](/wiki/2012_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Ice_Hockey_Tournament "2012 NCAA Men's Division I Ice Hockey Tournament") after his Bowling Green championship in [1984](/wiki/1984_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Ice_Hockey_Tournament "1984 NCAA Men's Division I Ice Hockey Tournament").[[165]](#cite_note-165)

### Club sports and events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Club sports and events")]

BGSU offers a variety of sports at the club level. Men's sports include cross country/track and field, rugby, lacrosse, baseball, basketball, soccer and ice hockey. Women's club sports include rugby, cross country/track and field, Lacrosse, soccer, softball, equestrian, figure skating, and gymnastics. BGSU also offers co-ed club sports, such as fencing. The club rugby team has been very successful, winning the Fall 2018 D1AA championship.[[166]](#cite_note-166) Also, in April 2011, BGSU hosted the National Club Track and Field Championships at Whittaker Track on the east end of campus.[[167]](#cite_note-167) BGSU has had an [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") team since 2014.[[168]](#cite_note-168)
