Ohio residents account for 85% of the undergraduate student population while out-of-state students come from all 50 US states and 70 foreign countries.[[170]](#cite_note-iesenrollment-173)[[171]](#cite_note-steadyenroll-174) The student body consists of 54% women and 46% men, of which, 22% are either of international origin or members of ethnic [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_groups "Minority groups").[[170]](#cite_note-iesenrollment-173) Bowling Green had an on-campus residential student population of 6,500 students as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit).[[172]](#cite_note-newdorms-175) Approximately 85% are in-state students.[[170]](#cite_note-iesenrollment-173) The majority of students attend classes on BGSU's main campus.[[170]](#cite_note-iesenrollment-173)[[171]](#cite_note-steadyenroll-174) In addition to the main campus enrollment, 2,500 students enrolled in classes at BGSU Firelands as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit). 300 students attend classes at BGSU extension locations, and over 600 students attend classes via distance learning.[[173]](#cite_note-176)[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Activities")]

#### BGeXperience

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: BGeXperience")]

In 2012 Bowling Green redesigned its undergraduate curriculum, creating an interdisciplinary program known as the BGeXperience (BGeX),[[175]](#cite_note-178) that places a focus on [personal growth and development](/wiki/Personal_development "Personal development"), [social connections](/wiki/Social_connection "Social connection"), [critical thinking](/wiki/Critical_thinking "Critical thinking"), [problem solving](/wiki/Problem_solving "Problem solving") and [diversity](/wiki/Diversity_training "Diversity training"). First-year students begin the BGeX program during the BGeX Introduction Weekend prior to the start of the semester and continued taking courses designed to meet BGeX criteria throughout the four years of the undergraduate programs.[[176]](#cite_note-recognition-179)

#### Outdoor Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Outdoor Program")]

As part of the Department of Recreational Sports, the BGSU Outdoor Program[[177]](#cite_note-outdoorprogram-180) offers outdoor trips, an indoor [climbing wall](/wiki/Climbing_wall "Climbing wall"), team-building, and an outdoor equipment rental center to BGSU students, faculty, staff and surrounding community members.[[177]](#cite_note-outdoorprogram-180) In August 2008 the program introduced the Freshman Wilderness Experience, which couples a week-long backpacking trip on the [Appalachian Trail](/wiki/Appalachian_Trail "Appalachian Trail") with a monthly class to assist students in transitioning from high school to college life.[[178]](#cite_note-181) In 2009 The Outdoor Program won the David J Web Award by the Association of Outdoor Recreation and Education (AORE) as an outstanding non-profit outdoor program.[[179]](#cite_note-182)

### Student organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Student organizations")]

The university has an extensive student life program, with over 300 student organizations; club and recreational sports programs; nationally ranked living-learning communities and [freshmen experience programs](/wiki/Freshmen_experience_programs "Freshmen experience programs"); student media organizations and publications; and [Greek](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") organizations.[[180]](#cite_note-183) In BGSU's residential learning communities, students with similar interests, majors, cultural connections, and goals live and study together. In the academically based communities students work closely with faculty members who teach classes and have offices in the residence hall.[[176]](#cite_note-recognition-179)

### Greek life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Greek life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Panhel-photo.jpg/250px-Panhel-photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panhel-photo.jpg)

Members of the College Panhellenic Conference gather outside of the Student Union.

In 2014, BGSU demolished its Greek Town Homes located on old Fraternity Row, old Sorority Row, and the North and South Greek Conklin buildings and finished construction of a new Greek Village in 2016.[[181]](#cite_note-184)[[182]](#cite_note-185) The current Greek Village consists of ten buildings divided into 33 sections.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

In 2021, Bowling Green student [Stone Foltz](/wiki/Death_of_Stone_Foltz "Death of Stone Foltz") died as a result of alcohol poisoning at the Delta Beta chapter of Pi Kappa Alpha International Fraternity. Two of the fraternity's members were found guilty for hazing Foltz and given jail sentences.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

### Media and publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Media and publications")]

#### Newspaper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Newspaper")]

Campus newspapers include the independent [student newspaper](/wiki/Student_newspaper "Student newspaper"), *[The BG News](/wiki/BG_News "BG News")*, published since 1920.[[185]](#cite_note-nwohpapers-188) It was known as the *Bee Gee News* before assuming its current name on September 21, 1951.[[185]](#cite_note-nwohpapers-188) The paper is available for free at 135 newsstands. In 2009 the paper became available on the Internet as an [online newspaper](/wiki/Online_newspaper "Online newspaper").[[186]](#cite_note-ratecard-189) The paper prints Monday and Thursday during the fall and spring semesters.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

The university's independent, student operated [yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook "Yearbook") was first published in 1918 but stopped after one edition for six years. In 1924 it resumed production and was published every year as a record of students, activities, and events for a given year.[[188]](#cite_note-thekey-191) In 2008, the yearbook was replaced with a magazine format, *The Key Magazine*, and is published semi-annually, in fall and spring semesters.[[188]](#cite_note-thekey-191)

#### Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/WBGU-TV_Brain_Set.jpg/250px-WBGU-TV_Brain_Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:WBGU-TV_Brain_Set.jpg)

Part of the WBGU-TV recording studio

[Electronic media](/wiki/Electronic_media "Electronic media") on campus includes two radio stations and one [public television](/wiki/Public_television "Public television") station, as well as student-produced television. BGSU's [Public Broadcasting Service](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") affiliate, [WBGU-TV](/wiki/WBGU-TV "WBGU-TV"), broadcasts to nineteen counties in northwestern and west central [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and hosts PBS programming, local programming and BG24 News, a student-run [television newscast](/wiki/Television_news "Television news") airing live at 5:30 pm twice a week. The campus is home to two student-operated radio stations as part of the Department of Telecommunications. [WBGU 88.1 FM](/wiki/WBGU-FM "WBGU-FM") and [WFAL Falcon Radio](/wiki/WFAL_Falcon_Radio "WFAL Falcon Radio"). WBGU-FM serves as an [independent radio](/wiki/Independent_radio "Independent radio"), [non-commercial educational](/wiki/Non-commercial_educational "Non-commercial educational") (NCE), FCC-licensed station that focuses mostly on [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") programming and broadcasts women's basketball and hockey;[[189]](#cite_note-wbgufm-192) while WFAL Falcon Radio, formerly WFAL 1610 AM, is a student-run [commercial radio](/wiki/Commercial_radio "Commercial radio") station that broadcasts music including [Modern rock](/wiki/Modern_rock "Modern rock"), [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40"), [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") and [talk shows](/wiki/Talk_shows "Talk shows").[[190]](#cite_note-193) Both radio stations host news and sports talk shows and BGSU athletic events through partnerships with other student-media organizations. The Bowling Green Radio News Organization (BGRNO) provides radio news coverage Monday–Friday through student-produced shows;[[191]](#cite_note-tcommorgs-194) while the Falcon Media Sports Network (FMSN) formally known as the Bowling Green Radio Sports Organization (BGRSO) broadcasts BGSU athletic events on WBGU-FM and WFAL as well as local high school sports on WFAL.[[191]](#cite_note-tcommorgs-194)

#### Journals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Journals")]

The *[Mid-American Review](/wiki/Mid-American_Review "Mid-American Review")* is an international [literary journal](/wiki/Literary_journal "Literary journal") published through the BGSU Department of English. The Mid-American Review showcases contemporary fiction, poetry, nonfiction, and translations.[[192]](#cite_note-195) It was created in 1980 when the format changed from a student-published literary magazine, known as the *Itinerary*, to an international publication.[[193]](#cite_note-196) *Prairie Margins* is a national undergraduate literary journal published by students in the Creative Writing Program. The annual journal features literary work by both BGSU students and undergraduate creative writers from other institutions.[[194]](#cite_note-197) *The Projector* is a peer-reviewed [electronic journal](/wiki/Electronic_journal "Electronic journal") on film, media and culture published twice a year by the Department of Theatre & Film.[[195]](#cite_note-198)

### Traditions and events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Traditions and events")]

#### Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Mascots")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/FriedaFalcon.jpg/250px-FriedaFalcon.jpg)](/wiki/File:FriedaFalcon.jpg)

Frieda Falcon at a basketball game

BGSU's official mascots are [Freddie and Frieda Falcon](/wiki/Freddie_and_Frieda_Falcon "Freddie and Frieda Falcon"). Freddie Falcon began appearing at athletic events in 1950, while Frieda first appeared on February 25, 1966, as Mrs. Freddie Falcon. Frieda returned in the 1980–81 academic year as Freddie's little sister. 1980 marked the first official female Frieda after the 1966 version was played by a male cheerleader. Freddie and Frieda routinely make appearances at BGSU athletic events and other major events around campus and the community. In a similar tradition to SICSIC, the identities of the students who play Freddie and Frieda are not revealed until the end of the basketball and hockey seasons.[[196]](#cite_note-birds-199)

SICSIC is an official spirit organization and [secret society](/wiki/Secret_society "Secret society") at BGSU that began in 1946 by President Frank J. Prout.[[153]](#cite_note-histandtraditions-153) SICSIC routinely attends major BGSU sporting events and other campus activities promoting school spirit. The organization is secret and contains six members, two each for sophomore, junior and senior classes. Two new members are chosen at the end of their class's freshman year to replace that year's two graduating seniors.[[153]](#cite_note-histandtraditions-153) The group is characterized by their use of gray [jumpsuits](/wiki/Jumpsuit "Jumpsuit") and masks of famous pop culture and political figures to hide their identities, which are not revealed until the last home basketball game of their senior year.[[197]](#cite_note-200)

#### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Events")]

BGSU currently is the home of Ohio's largest student-run philanthropy, Dance Marathon.[[198]](#cite_note-dance-201) BGSU's chapter is one of the largest and most active Dance Marathon organizations in the nation.[[198]](#cite_note-dance-201) BGSU Dance Marathon operates similar to other [dance marathon](/wiki/Dance_marathon "Dance marathon") events held nationwide at other college. The event is run entirely by college students and the proceeds go to local children's hospitals. Funds raised through BGSU's Dance Marathon benefit children at the Mercy Children's Hospital in [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio") via the [Children's Miracle Network](/wiki/Children%27s_Miracle_Network "Children's Miracle Network").[[198]](#cite_note-dance-201) 2011 marked the 16th year the BG has conducted the Dance Marathon.[[199]](#cite_note-dancerecord-202) Since the event began, it has raised over $2.2 million for the Children's Hospital.[[199]](#cite_note-dancerecord-202)

Bowling Green hosts an annual event for three days in February similar to other [winter cities](/wiki/Winter_City "Winter City") to celebrate winter, snow, and [cold weather activities](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports").[[200]](#cite_note-203) Winterfest was first held in 2009 and centers around the town's rich [ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating") and [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") traditions.[[201]](#cite_note-hamiltonreturns-204) Winterfest events are held all over Bowling Green.[[202]](#cite_note-205) Many of the on campus events are held at the [Slater Family Ice Arena](/wiki/Slater_Family_Ice_Arena "Slater Family Ice Arena"), including curling, skating, [ice hockey](/wiki/Bowling_Green_Falcons_men%27s_ice_hockey "Bowling Green Falcons men's ice hockey") and figure skating exhibitions.[[203]](#cite_note-206)

Bowling Green hosted the [American Legion](/wiki/American_Legion "American Legion") event known as Buckeye Boys State from 1978 to 2016.[[204]](#cite_note-207)[[205]](#cite_note-208) The program gathered high school juniors from all over [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") for a nine-day program in June. At Buckeye Boys State, the students operated a full government modeled after the [Government of Ohio](/wiki/Government_of_Ohio "Government of Ohio").[[206]](#cite_note-209)

### Marching band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bowling_Green_State_University&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Marching band")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Falcon_Marching_Band.jpg/250px-Falcon_Marching_Band.jpg)](/wiki/File:Falcon_Marching_Band.jpg)

The [Falcon Marching Band](/wiki/Falcon_Marching_Band "Falcon Marching Band") performing at [Doyt Perry Stadium](/wiki/Doyt_Perry_Stadium "Doyt Perry Stadium")

The [Falcon Marching Band](/wiki/Falcon_Marching_Band "Falcon Marching Band") is the largest student organization at BGSU, with approximately 425 members in 2024.[[207]](#cite_note-fmb-210) The band performs at home football games and other university functions including select road football games and various [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_games "Bowl games") including the [Raisin Bowl](/wiki/Raisin_Bowl "Raisin Bowl") in [Fresno, California](/wiki/Fresno,_California "Fresno, California"), [Silver Bowl](/wiki/Silver_Bowl "Silver Bowl") in [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"), the [Motor City Bowl](/wiki/Motor_City_Bowl "Motor City Bowl") in [Detroit, Michigan](/wiki/Detroit,_Michigan "Detroit, Michigan"), the [GMAC Bowl](/wiki/GMAC_Bowl "GMAC Bowl") in [Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Mobile,_Alabama "Mobile, Alabama"), the [Humanitarian Bowl](/wiki/Humanitarian_Bowl "Humanitarian Bowl") in [Boise, Idaho](/wiki/Boise,_Idaho "Boise, Idaho"), the [Military Bowl](/wiki/Military_Bowl "Military Bowl") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and the [Little Caesars Pizza Bowl](/wiki/Little_Caesars_Pizza_Bowl "Little Caesars Pizza Bowl") in Detroit.[[207]](#cite_note-fmb-210) In 2007, the band was invited to the [Bands of America](/wiki/Bands_of_America "Bands of America") Regional in [Indianapolis, Indiana](/wiki/Indianapolis,_Indiana "Indianapolis, Indiana").[[207]](#cite_note-fmb-210) The Athletic Band is an auditioned group that at most basketball and hockey games as well as other university events.[[207]](#cite_note-fmb-210)

The first band to represent what would become Bowling Green State University was formed during the 1923–1924 academic year. Making its first appearance early during the football season, the band's premier performance that year as at the dedication of the new athletic field at Homecoming. The Falcon Marching Band, features a symphonic sound and chair step marching that rivals bands of larger conferences. The 250-member marching band is the largest student organization on campus. The band performs at all home football games, which are hosted in Doyt Perry Stadium as well as other various university functions. The Falcon Marching Band only exists during the football season.[[208]](#cite_note-211)
